perturbation.original_text: At any time, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all copies of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials shall be returned to the Disclosing Party within five (5) business days of such request; provided, however, that the R eceiving Party shall be permitted to retain a list that contains general descriptions of the docum ents it has returned to the Disclosing Party to facilitate the resolution of any controversies after the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials have been returned.
perturbation.changed_text: At any time, at the Disclosing Party’s request, all copies of th e Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials shall be returned to the Disclosing Party within one (1) business day of such request; provided, however, that the R eceiving Party shall be permitted to retain a list that contains general descriptions of the docum ents it has returned to the Disclosing Party to facilitate the resolution of any controversies after the Confidential Information and the Confidential Materials have been returned.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text reduces the return period for confidential information to one business day, which may be unreasonably short, particularly given the volume and complexity of the materials. This can be seen as a breach of good faith, because it is nearly impossible to return large volumes of information in such short time. Florida Statute 671.304 states that every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification violates Florida Statute 671.304 because requiring return of all confidential information within one business day imposes an unreasonable burden on the Receiving Party, potentially preventing them from fulfilling their obligations in good faith. Good faith is defined as 'honesty in fact and the observance of reasonable commercial standards of fair dealing.' A one-day turnaround for potentially voluminous documents doesn't meet this standard.
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text reduces the time for returning confidential information to one business day. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation assert that this timeframe is unreasonably short and could constitute a breach of good faith under Florida Statute 671.304, as it places an undue burden on the Receiving Party. The absence of specific law snippets doesn't negate the provided reasoning, which explicitly links the modification to a potential violation of good faith obligations under the referenced statute. The change directly affects a time-sensitive obligation, making it potentially unenforceable due to its impracticality, and creating an inconsistency that falls under the given definition.

perturbation.original_text: The Receiving Party shall also, within ten (10) days of a written re quest by the Disclosing Part y, certify in writing that it has satisfied its obligations unde r this Section 6 or explain w hy the Receiving Party has been unable to do so.
perturbation.changed_text: The Receiving Party shall also, within one (1) day of a written request by the Disclosing Part y, certify in writing that it has satisfied its obligations unde r this Section 6 or explain w hy the Receiving Party has been unable to do so.
perturbation.explanation: The modified text requires the Receiving Party to provide written certification of compliance within one day, which is an unreasonably short timeframe. Florida Statute 671.304 states that every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts Florida Statute 671.304 because demanding certification of compliance with document return obligations within one day is commercially unreasonable and might not allow for the Receiving Party to be honest in fact and observe reasonable standards of fair dealing, thus violating the implied duty of good faith.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract sets a deadline of one day for compliance certification, which the perturbation explanation argues is unreasonably short and contradicts the implied duty of good faith under Florida Statute 671.304. The law explanation supports this by stating that such a short timeframe might not allow for honesty and fair dealing. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 is a 404 error, indicating the page was not found, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation highlight a contradiction based on the principle of good faith, suggesting the one-day deadline is commercially unreasonable. This unreasonable deadline imposed by the changed text directly contradicts the obligation of good faith in the performance of contracts.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that the Receiving Party is issued any subpoe na or other process requesting any of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials, the Receiving Party agrees that it will immediately furnish a copy of such subpoena or other process, via overnight delivery, to counsel to the Assignee, Edward J. Peterson, Esq. and Matthew B. Hale, Esq. of Stichter, Riedel, 5 Blain & Postler, P.A., 110 East Madison Street , Suite 200, Tampa, Florida 33602, and to the Disclosing Party at the address set forth on Exhibit B attached to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that the Receiving Party is issued any subpoe na or other process requesting any of the Confidential Information or the Confidential Materials, the Receiving Party agrees that it will furnish a copy of such subpoena or other process when reasonably possible, to counsel to the Assignee, Edward J. Peterson, Esq. and Matthew B. Hale, Esq. of Stichter, Riedel, 5 Blain & Postler, P.A., 110 East Madison Street , Suite 200, Tampa, Florida 33602, and to the Disclosing Party at the address set forth on Exhibit B attached to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes 'immediately furnish a copy via overnight delivery' to 'furnish a copy when reasonably possible'. This could violate Florida Statute § 48.031, which outlines the requirements for service of process. Failing to immediately notify the Disclosing Party and its counsel through overnight delivery could hinder their ability to respond to the subpoena in a timely fashion.
perturbation.law_explanation: By changing the requirement to 'when reasonably possible', the Receiving Party might delay notification, thus impeding the Disclosing Party's ability to seek a protective order. Florida Statute § 48.031 deals with how process is to be served, and while this NDA isn't the same as actual process, it is related to legal procedures surrounding a subpoena, and delaying notification would make the statute difficult to adhere to.

Home > Laws > 2011 Florida Statutes > Title VI > Chapter 48 > Section 031 Quick Links General Laws Conversion Table (2024) [PDF] Florida Statutes Definitions Index (2024) [PDF] Table of Section Changes (2024) [PDF] Preface to the Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Table Tracing Session Laws to Florida Statutes (2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1971-2024) [PDF] Index to Special and Local Laws (1845-1970) [PDF] Statute Search Tips 2011 Florida Statutes Title VI CIVIL PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE Chapter 48 PROCESS AND SERVICE OF PROCESS Entire Chapter SECTION 031 Service of process generally; service of witness subpoenas. F.S. 48.031 48.031 Service of process generally; service of witness subpoenas. — (1)(a) Service of original process is made by delivering a copy of it to the person to be served with a copy of the complaint, petition, or other initial pleading or paper or by leaving the copies at his or her usual place of abode with any person residing therein who is 15 years of age or older and informing the person of their contents. Minors who are or have been married shall be served as provided in this section. (b) Employers, when contacted by an individual authorized to make service of process, shall permit the authorized individual to make service on employees in a private area designated by the employer. (2)(a) Substitute service may be made on the spouse of the person to be served at any place in the county, if the cause of action is not an adversary proceeding between the spouse and the person to be served, if the spouse requests such service, and if the spouse and person to be served are residing together in the same dwelling. (b) Substitute service may be made on an individual doing business as a sole proprietorship at his or her place of business, during regular business hours, by serving the person in charge of the business at the time of service if two or more attempts to serve the owner have been made at the place of business. (3)(a) The service of process of witness subpoenas, whether in criminal cases or civil actions, shall be made as provided in subsection (1). However, service of a subpoena on a witness in a criminal traffic case, a misdemeanor case, or a second degree or third degree felony may be made by United States mail directed to the witness at the last known address, and the service must be mailed at least 7 days prior to the date of the witness’s required appearance. Failure of a witness to appear in response to a subpoena served by United States mail that is not certified may not be grounds for finding the witness in contempt of court. (b) A criminal witness subpoe
perturbation.justification: The original contract mandated 'immediately furnish a copy via overnight delivery,' while the changed text states 'furnish a copy when reasonably possible.' This modification introduces a significant inconsistency regarding the timeliness of notification, potentially contradicting the intent behind proper legal procedures, though not directly violating the statute. The scraped snippet of Florida Statute § 48.031 discusses service of process, requiring a copy of the process to be delivered to the person to be served. While the contract pertains to the process of notification related to a subpoena rather than the subpoena itself, delaying this notification impacts the ability of the Disclosing Party to respond appropriately to the subpoena, thus creating a contradiction by significantly weakening the original obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise set forth in this Agreement, this Agreement shall terminate on the earlier to occur of: (i) the consummation of a Possi ble Transaction pursuant to a 8 Definitive Agreement, and (ii) two (2) years fr om the date that the Parties terminate their discussions and consideration of a Possible Transaction; provide d, however, that all Confidential Information and Confidential Material s shall continue to be subject to the terms of this Agreement after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise set forth in this Agreement, this Agreement shall terminate on the earlier to occur of: (i) the consummation of a Possi ble Transaction pursuant to a Definitive Agreement, and (ii) six (6) months fr om the date that the Parties terminate their discussions and consideration of a Possible Transaction; provide d, however, that all Confidential Information and Confidential Material s shall continue to be subject to the terms of this Agreement after the termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination period to six months is unreasonable. This might be argued as an unconscionable term, especially if the confidential information has a longer lifespan of value. While Florida doesn't have a specific statute defining unconscionability in all contract types, courts apply common law principles. A contract is unconscionable if it is both procedurally and substantively unconscionable.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Florida Common Law Principles of Unconscionability
perturbation.law_citation: Florida Common Law Principles of Unconscionability
perturbation.law_explanation: While there's no single statute, Florida common law, as exemplified in cases like *Kohl v. Bay Colony Club Condo., Inc.*, requires that contracts be fair and not unconscionable. Reducing the protection period of confidential information to six months could be deemed substantively unconscionable if the information retains significant value beyond that period, especially when combined with a power imbalance that could make the term procedurally unconscionable as well.

perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the termination period for confidential information protection from two years to six months. The Perturbation and Law Explanations argue that this could be deemed unconscionable under Florida common law, especially if the information retains value beyond six months. While the scraped snippets are unhelpful, the inconsistency created by reducing the protection period may violate the principle of fairness in contracts, potentially rendering the clause unenforceable if a court finds it substantively and procedurally unconscionable.
